sentences.text: Orphanages in those days were places that you said, yes, certainly.
You didn't ask for anything.
If you were my age, you were in charge of the rest of your siblings.
You had no clothes.
You had nothing personal of your own-- we didn't have it anyway-- because everything was communal.
On Friday evening, before Shabbos, we would go to the stockroom.
And we were handed the clothes for the next week.
There were clothes for Shabbos, which we then put away.
And we had-- nothing belonged to us.
And we cooked, and we cleaned, and we polished.
And we looked after our own.

But the worst part was where there is plenty of money from the community-- by the time I'm talking, that's 1937-38, and the laws had already come in that Jewish people were only allowed to buy in one shop.
The Nuremberg laws.
And I didn't know these laws.
I knew we couldn't do it.
But the difficult part was to hear your own family-- my children-- I can only-- I've always called them my children and I can't help it.
My children never cried unless they were hungry.
And when they cried they were hungry, I couldn't tolerate this.
So after a while, I went to the superintendent.
[CRYING] Excuse me.
[CRYING]
I must explain first that I had long blond plaits and blue eyes.
And my brother also was fair.
We didn't look very Jewish.
And I decided that we were going shopping.
So I went to the superintendent and said, please can I have some money?
They wanted to know why.
I said, my children are hungry.
I want to go shopping.
So we did.
We went to different parts of town.
Because normally we weren't allowed out of the orphanage gates.
We went to different parts of town and we shopped.
And there are two little children with an enormous basket full of necessities of life.
And we knew the necessities.
And we got away with it for about two months.
It then we suddenly found we were followed by Nazi youths.
And they caught us one day, and beat us, and took our basket, and literally threw us into the gates of the orphanage, and called us all the vile names they could lay tongue to.
And the superintendent said, you know, that's it.
No more.
And for a while, that was it, and no more, until I could no longer bear to hear my children crying.
And I went to the superintendent.
[CRYING] Excuse me.
And I said, please can I have some money?
Oh, no, she said.
You're not going out again.
And I said, if you don't give me money, I will steal it.
And I was a very obstinate child and I still am.
And she looked me straight in the eye and said, I believe you would.
She gave me money.
We went out to the little gate on the other side.
But we didn't get away with it for very long.
After a few weeks, somebody must have followed us again.
This time we were caught by the Gestapo and beaten with [? trunchons. ?] [CRYING] I'm sorry.
My brother was eight and I was nine.
And ever since, I've had two faulty kidneys [CRYING] from being beaten.
[CRYING] Grown men beating eight, nine-year-olds, as if they were grown-ups.
[CRYING] I'm sorry.
[CRYING] I'm afraid this is something my family doesn't know.
But that was the last time-- the last time we were ever allowed out.
And I had to put up with my children crying because there was no way on anybody's Earth that I was allowed out again.
And life carried on like this.
We had classes in the classrooms, in the orphanage, to continue our Hebrew.
And the boys-- mainly the boys-- were given classes in everything else, because we girls were too busy.
We had to run [INAUDIBLE].
And we had to cook, and clean, and polish.
Until one day, a photographer came to the orphanage.
As I said, in those days, one didn't ask why.
And someone said, smile.
I have nothing to smile about, but you put a smile on your face.
I've still got the photograph.
And I don't know how many photographs were taken.
I have no idea.
But I know mine was.
And we heard nothing for a while.
And then I was called to the hall.
And then these orphanages are absolutely enormous places.
And for a child-- I mean, in hindsight, they were big.
For a child, they were unbelievable.
And I was called to the hall-- no telephone, you just send another child.
And the superintendent sent for me.
And I came down these massive steps.
I could see somebody or something sitting in a chair.
And I got closer and closer-- I still didn't know who or what it was-- until I could completely [INAUDIBLE] next to him.
[CRYING] I'm sorry.
My father was a big man with blond, curly hair.
And what I was looking at was a skeleton in clothes with no hair.
[CRYING] This was my father, [CRYING] the handsomest man I've ever known.
This skeleton was my father.
He'd been allowed out.
Sonny, could you get me a tissue, please?
I just don't seem to have any left.
He was allowed out to get papers.
My father didn't explain no more than the orphanage did, except that I had to go with him for days at a time--
--to different offices.
And eventually-- as I said, I had no idea what was happening.
Eventually, we arrived at the station, the train station.
Yes, my father was with us.
The skeleton was with us.
And when you live in a village as small as Hoof, if you don't know [? what ?] a train is, you've never seen it-- I had never seen a train, which was an enormous thing.
My mother-- again, pregnant, and with this babe-- hugged and kissed me.
And she was crying.
And for the second time, I saw my father cry.
And he hugged me and kissed me, opened the door of this train carriage, put me inside, and locked the door.
The only memory I have was of this train receding and mother and father getting smaller.
[CRYING] I don't remember-- I don't how many more hundreds of children who came on this train.
It went all through Germany, picking up children.
Eventually we arrived at Hamburg.
And you were the only sibling from your family?
Yes, the only one out of six.
We arrived at Hamburg.
And I had never seen water bigger than a pond.
And I saw this enormous mass of water with a floating house on it.
I had never seen even a picture of a boat.
We were put aboard the boat-- ship.
I don't know what it's called.
And the next thing I remember, I was arriving at Southampton.
We couldn't have been very long, because my exit certificate said June 29th and I arrived in England on the 30th.
And again, there was a train once we disembarked in Southampton.
No.
I was-- I had no memory of anything.
All I could see was my parents' faces.
And all I wanted was my brothers and sisters.
Because they followed us from the orphanage.
Because they were always following everybody.
Anyway, we arrived in Southampton.
And there was another enormous train.
And on each seat of this train, there were little boxes.
And I vividly remember at home, the only food you ate was what you picked off trees.
I had never seen a banana.
And there was I, trying to eat this banana with the skin on, because I had no idea what it was supposed to do.
And there was an orange.
And I was trying to eat that.
And people came along and showed us what to do.
And another long journey, and this time we arrived in London.
It was absolutely terrifying.
I knew nobody else.
And we all had big labels on us, with our names on it.
And we arrived at--
No.
No one explained anything.
No one ever explained anything.
In those-- in those days, one did not explain to a child.
And I was supposedly a child.
And at 10, my father was too busy trying to cope, my mother trying to cope with a babe-- again, very pregnant.
And the orphanage would tell you nothing at all.
We arrived in this enormous, enormous hall in London, which was set in tiers-- you know, platform.
And we were all sort of spread out.
And as your name was called, you'd have to go all the way down all these steps and meet a complete stranger.
Now with my luck, my name was Wertheim, so I here I was tail end again.
I walked-- my name was called.
I walked down these steps.
And I looked up at this man.
To me he looked about eight-foot tall.
And he held out his hand for me.
And I put mine in his.
And we walked away.
He couldn't speak a word of German and I couldn't speak a word of English.
We got to Paddington, which is the station to bring me to Swansea, where I eventually lived.
And he was so desperate.
He didn't know what to do.
And there was a little kiosk who sold sweets and chocolates.
And there were some peaches.
Now, I knew about peaches, so I pointed to the peaches.
And we went and bought such an enormous bag of peaches.
On the journey from London to Swansea, where I eventually lived, in those days took about six and 1/2 hours.
And this poor man didn't know what to do, so he kept on feeding me peaches and wiping the juice off my chin.
And then I would smile at him, because I didn't know what to say or do.
And this poor man and I sat for six and 1/2 hours with not one word between us.
I arrived in Swansea.
We arrived in Swansea.
It appears my photograph-- I must explain this.
My photograph, with many others, had been sent all over the world.
It went-- came to America, Canada, you name it, these photographs had been sent.
And these people-- Jewish people from Swansea-- the man was 70.
The lady was 50.
They had no children.
And they wanted someone to look after them in their old age.
This is why they chose a 10-year-old.
It was very, very hard.
This was called the Kindertransport.
And of all the countries, large and small, only Great Britain-- which was the smallest of all-- took 10,000 children between 1938 and 1939.
And I arrived in June 30, 1939.
But only Great Britain took 10,000 children.
Now, to return to my foster parents-- when a Jewish lady doesn't know what to do with a child-- I think every mother is the same-- they feed it.
So she put all this food in front of me.
And I mean, I hadn't eaten a meal for God knows how long.
A meal-- you know, a meal in the orphanage was sour milk with sugar and cinnamon.
That was a meal.
And she was very distressed because I just couldn't eat this food, which was so foreign to me.
I possibly nibbled on a bread.
But I mean, that's about all I could cope with.
And I could see her getting more and more upset.
But there was nothing I could do about it.
Anyway, I think we both gave in, and I went to bed.
You have to understand that this lady, at 60-- at 50, had never had children, had no idea how to treat a child.
Next day, my-- I had never been to a hairdresser.
I had never had my hair cut.
And my plaits had saved my life many times.
The first thing she did was she took me to a hairdresser and had my plaits cut off.
And the second thing she did-- my second name is Kerry.
And in Germany, I had no other name.
I was always Kerry.
And she didn't like it.
So she decided to choose my other name, which was Ellen.
I had never, ever been called Ellen.
So every time she called me, I didn't answer.
Every time I looked in a mirror, I'm looking at a stranger.
My plaits have gone.
My name has gone.
I'm a complete stranger, amongst strangers.
I can't talk to them.
I can't explain to them how I feel because I have no English and they have no German.
This impasse lasted for about three weeks, by which time I had learned to eat.
And I had learned how not to displease the lady.
But I had not learned any English.
So after three weeks, they decided to send me to a school.
This was a normal primary school.
And I think I was very fortunate.
I had a teacher who didn't speak a word of English, but had patience.
And so much patience--
No.
She spoke-- I apologize.
She spoke English.
I spoke German.
But she had no idea what I was talking about.
And I had no idea what she was talking about.
But she was so patient.
How I learned English, I don't know.
I can only find one word for it and that's osmosis.
You learn it through the skin.
So I learned English.
My foster father was kindness itself.
Their names were Feigenbaum.
Heiman and Sarah.
And at 70, he found joy in me, whereas she found me a nuisance.
And I loved him very dearly.
Unfortunately, he died within five years.
But I was in primary school for-- from the age of 10 and 1/2 to 12, and by which time I spoke English.
And they decided that I should go to a commercial college.
So I went to a commercial college.
I have never been brilliant, but when you have someone standing over you saying, we are paying for this, you work.
And I worked very hard.
And by the time I was 15, I had every diploma, every certificate, every qualification you could name.
We lived in the center of Swansea.
My foster father made trimmings for dress makers-- very well known everywhere.
But what the Germans couldn't do to me in Germany, they tried to do to me in Swansea.
And they bombed our house.
And the only thing I had was a little suitcase and a photograph of my parents.
And everything was destroyed.
But the main thing I need to tell was that when I was 10 and 1/2, I had a letter.
I'd only ever had one card from my mother.
And that was in August, 1940-- no '39-- to tell me I had a sister, whose name was Zeilla.
And I didn't hear anything after that.
January, February '42, I had a letter from the Red Cross to say that all my family had been sent to Riga-- my mother, four brothers, and two sisters.
And as far as the Red Cross were concerned, they were dead.
They didn't say how they died, because they didn't know.
Maybe-- maybe a week after I received that letter, the bomb came, smashed our house, and the house underneath, and it just about missed me.
The letter was destroyed.
And like every other normal child, I tried to erase this from my memory.
It was bad enough having to leave them, but to know they'd all died-- this I couldn't live with.
And I couldn't acknowledge-- excuse me.
[DRINKS] For the moment I must return to my father, because he played another large part in my life.
I had always believed-- because within two months, he arrived in England.
After I arrived.
And for years, and years, and years, I believed he'd escaped from Dachau and come to England.
Yes.
What I didn't know was that he'd never gone back after getting my papers.
He'd realized that there was no hope of getting my mother or brothers and sisters out.
And I believe my mother persuaded him that if he could get out, he should.
And he just took off.
How he escaped, he would never speak of.
He arrived in England.
He was interned as an alien.
He joined the Pioneer Corps.
And he came to see me on embarkation leave-- now in the Army-- because he was being sent to Australia on the most dreadful ship I have ever read about.
And that was called the Dunera.
And anyone who knows anything about people who went to Australia will know of the Dunera with terror.
It was a ship manned by British soldiers and sailors.
And they were almost as bad as the Germans.
They took every stitch these soldiers had, and that wasn't much.
The soldiers I mean like my father, who joined the Pioneer Corps.
They were interned in Australia, into another camp.
And eventually, after the war, when the war was finishing, they were demobbed.
And my father-- demobbed means coming out of the forces.
And my father decide to stay in Australia, knowing that my mother had died and the other children had died.
And we corresponded for a while.
And then-- it's another story because it came much later.
I stayed-- well, I stayed with my foster parents.
My foster father died when I was 15.
And I had to take over his business at 15 because my foster mother had never worked.
She had a maid.
And my foster father never did-- let her do anything-- never do anything except look pretty, and go play whist-- no, bridge.
Bridge.
I hate bridge.
It has bad connotations for me.
But I had to take over a business and a workshop because there were people making these goods.
And these girls-- as they were known-- were old enough to be my mother.
So I would run the business-- I mean run, from stock taking, to seeing to customers, to doing the books.
And then I would go home to wash, clean, polish, bake and cook.
And that lasted until we couldn't bear one another anymore.
Because whenever we had a disagreement, which was about once a week, she'd turn round to me and say, if it wasn't for me, you'd be in the gas chambers with your brothers and sisters.
She once called my father a Nazi and I threw a plate at her.
[LAUGHS] I missed.
But she was not a nice person.
She really wasn't.
And eventually I couldn't-- I couldn't take this any longer.
And very foolishly, I married her nephew.
I was 19.
And because I was underage, I had to get permission from my father.
We were still corresponding and I wrote to ask him for permission.
He gave permission and said he had just remarried.
That was the last I'd ever heard from him.
I sent him letters and photographs of my children.
I had them delivered by hand.
And I know he received them but he never, ever contacted me for the rest of my life.
No.
He had no more children.
And he died in 1976, 20 years ago.
I think he was only in his 50s-- no, early 60s, I'm sorry.
I'm trying to work out-- because he was just 22 years older than I, 22 years and eight days.
And I never heard from him again, despite all the letters, and all the correspondence, and everything else.
So this meant that I had lost everybody, including my father.
Oh, his name was Lewis, Sydney Lewis.
It was a marriage made in hell.
I was a true orphan.
But the only thing that came out of this marriage were two wonderful children.
I lost two children.
I had two miscarriages because of my kidneys.
Very little-- as little as possible, the only Jewish man I've ever known who was work shy.
He just didn't like working.
Oh, yes, I had to work.
I had to work and bring up a family.
I had to pay a mortgage, as we call it, on the house.
I had to feed my family.
Because I-- originally, I couldn't go out to work.
I used to do invisible mending at home.
